diagrams.title: Tomcat Server Architecture
diagrams.description: A hierarchical block diagram showing Tomcat's container architecture and request processing pipeline
elements: Engine container (Catalina) - top-level container managing the entire server
Host container - represents a virtual host (e.g., localhost)
Context container - represents a single web application (e.g., /MyWebApp)
Wrapper container - represents a single servlet
Connector components - HTTP connectors listening on ports 8080, 8443
Request flow arrows: Connector → Engine → Host → Context → Wrapper → Servlet
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how Tomcat organizes web applications hierarchically and how requests are routed to the appropriate servlet through the container hierarchy.

diagrams.title: Web Application Directory Structure
diagrams.description: A tree diagram showing the complete directory structure of a deployed web application
elements: MyWebApp.war (or directory) at root
WEB-INF/ folder (not publicly accessible) with web.xml inside
WEB-INF/classes/ folder containing compiled .class servlet files
WEB-INF/lib/ folder containing JAR dependencies
index.html (public landing page)
style.css (public stylesheet)
script.js (public JavaScript)
images/ folder with assets
*.jsp files in root
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the standard directory structure required for Java web applications, understanding which files are publicly accessible and which are protected within WEB-INF.

diagrams.title: Servlet Request-Response Flow in Tomcat
diagrams.description: A flowchart showing step-by-step how Tomcat processes an HTTP request to a servlet
elements: Client sends HTTP request to http://localhost:8080/MyWebApp/login
Tomcat Connector receives request on port 8080
Connector creates Request and Response objects
Request flows through: Engine → Host → Context (MyWebApp)
Tomcat reads web.xml to find servlet mapping for /login
Context classloader loads HelloServlet.class from WEB-INF/classes/
Container creates servlet instance (if not already created)
Service method invoked with Request and Response objects
Servlet processes request, writes to Response
Container sends Response back to Client via Connector
HTTP response displayed in browser
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the complete request processing pipeline and how Tomcat coordinates the servlet lifecycle components.

diagrams.title: WAR File Deployment Process
diagrams.description: A sequential diagram showing the WAR deployment workflow using Tomcat Manager
elements: Developer creates WAR file (MyWebApp.war) in webapps/ directory
Tomcat's WARDeployer detects new WAR file
WAR file is extracted to webapps/MyWebApp/ directory
Tomcat reads WEB-INF/web.xml for servlet mappings
Servlet class files loaded into JVM classloader
Application becomes accessible at /MyWebApp
Manager application shows deployed application status
Undeploy process: deleting WAR file removes the application
diagrams.purpose: Students learn the automated deployment process, understanding how Tomcat extracts WAR files and makes web applications available for testing.

diagrams.title: Tomcat IDE Integration Setup
diagrams.description: A configuration diagram showing how Eclipse/IntelliJ integrates with Tomcat for development
elements: IDE window with project structure
Server Runtime Libraries (servlet-api.jar, jsp-api.jar)
Project facet configuration for Dynamic Web Project
Tomcat server adapter in IDE preferences
Deployment assembly mapping source folders to WEB-INF/classes
Debug mode with hot-swap capability
Console output showing server logs
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how to configure their development environment for efficient servlet development with real-time debugging capabilities.

charts.title: Tomcat Directory Structure Comparison
charts.content: CATALINA_HOME vs CATALINA_BASE: CATALINA_HOME contains bin/ (startup.sh, catalina.sh), conf/ (server.xml, web.xml), lib/ (servlet-api.jar), logs/. CATALINA_BASE contains conf/ (custom server config), logs/ (application logs), webapps/ (deployed applications), work/ (compiled JSPs), temp/ (temporary files). Each running instance can have its own BASE while sharing HOME.

charts.title: WAR Deployment Methods Comparison
charts.content: Method 1: Auto-deploy - Copy MyWebApp.war to webapps/ folder, Tomcat auto-extracts and deploys. Method 2: Manager App - Upload WAR through web interface at /manager/html. Method 3: IDE Deployment - Right-click project → Run on Server, IDE handles deployment. Method 4: Ant/Maven - Use build tools to deploy via Tomcat Manager API.

charts.title: Servlet Initialization Flow
charts.content: 1. Tomcat starts → 2. Loads web.xml → 3. For each <servlet>: a. Load servlet class (Class.forName), b. Create servlet instance (newInstance), c. Call init(ServletConfig) method → 4. Servlet ready to handle requests → 5. On first request: create Request/Response → 6. Call service() → 7. Process doGet/doPost → 8. Return Response → 9. Servlet instance remains loaded until shutdown or undeploy.

charts.title: web.xml Essential Elements Reference
charts.content: <display-name>My Web Application</display-name> - Application name. <servlet> - Defines servlet with <servlet-name> and <servlet-class>. <servlet-mapping> - Maps URL pattern to servlet with <url-pattern>. <welcome-file-list> - Specifies default files. <error-page> - Custom error handling. <session-config> - Session timeout in minutes. <welcome-file>index.jsp</welcome-file> - Default file.

branches: Server Components: Engine (Catalina), Host (Virtual Host), Context (Web App), Wrapper (Servlet)
Directory Structure: webapps/, WEB-INF/, classes/, lib/, web.xml
Deployment: WAR files, Auto-deploy, Manager App, IDE integration
Configuration: server.xml, context.xml, web.xml, server runtime
Request Processing: Connectors, Servlet lifecycle, Response flow
Development Tools: Eclipse/IntelliJ, Tomcat adapter, Debug mode, Hot deployment
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview of all components involved in servlet development using Tomcat, connecting theoretical knowledge to practical development environment.

branches: WAR File: .war archive, contains WEB-INF/ and public resources
Directory Structure: Standard layout with protected and public areas
Deployment Descriptor: web.xml, defines servlets and mappings
Lifecycle: Install → Start → Request handling → Stop → Undeploy
Tomcat Manager: Web UI for deploy/undeploy, view sessions, reload
IDE Integration: Eclipse WTP, IntelliJ run configuration, debugging
Class Loading: Delegation parent-first, WEB-INF/classes precedence
mindMaps.purpose: Maps out the complete deployment workflow and all configuration elements needed for successful servlet application deployment.
